name: Optimize tsconfig for Modern Projects
description: Configure tsconfig.json with the best compiler options for modern TypeScript projects targeting Node.js, React, or library development.
trendingReason: New moduleResolution and module options in TypeScript 5+ require updated configs

steps.title: Generate a starting tsconfig
steps.description: Use tsc --init to create a tsconfig.json with all options documented.

steps.title: Apply modern recommended settings
steps.description: Set the target, module, and resolution options for a modern Node.js or Next.js project.
steps.command:
cat > tsconfig.json << 'EOF'
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2022",
    "lib": ["ES2022", "DOM", "DOM.Iterable"],
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "resolveJsonModule": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,
    "strict": true,
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true,
    "noEmit": true,
    "jsx": "react-jsx",
    "incremental": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "paths": {
      "@/*": ["./src/*"]
    }
  },
  "include": ["src", "next-env.d.ts"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules", "dist"]
}
EOF
steps.tip: Use moduleResolution: 'bundler' for projects bundled with Vite, Webpack, or Next.js. Use 'nodenext' for pure Node.js projects.

steps.title: Enable stricter optional checks
steps.description: Add extra type safety options that go beyond strict: true.
steps.command: echo 'Add these to compilerOptions for maximum safety:' && echo '  "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true' && echo '  "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true' && echo '  "noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature": true'

steps.title: Verify the configuration
steps.description: Run the compiler to check for any configuration issues.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# TypeScript Config Optimization
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== TypeScript Version ==="
npx tsc --version

echo ""
echo "=== Recommended tsconfig.json ==="
cat << 'EOF'
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    // Language & Environment
    "target": "ES2022",
    "lib": ["ES2022", "DOM", "DOM.Iterable"],
    "jsx": "react-jsx",

    // Modules
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "resolveJsonModule": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,

    // Strictness
    "strict": true,
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true,
    "noImplicitOverride": true,

    // Output
    "noEmit": true,
    "incremental": true,

    // Interop
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,

    // Paths
    "baseUrl": ".",
    "paths": { "@/*": ["./src/*"] }
  },
  "include": ["src"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules", "dist"]
}
EOF

echo ""
echo "=== Verifying config ==="
npx tsc --noEmit

faq.question: What is the difference between moduleResolution bundler and nodenext?
faq.answer: Use 'bundler' when your code is processed by a bundler like Vite, Webpack, or Next.js, as it allows extensionless imports and condition-based resolution. Use 'nodenext' for pure Node.js projects that run directly without a bundler, which requires file extensions in imports.

faq.question: Should I use noEmit or emit declaration files?
faq.answer: Use noEmit: true when your bundler (Vite, Next.js, etc.) handles transpilation. Only emit when building a library that needs .js and .d.ts output files. For libraries, use declaration: true and declarationMap: true.

faq.question: What does isolatedModules do?
faq.answer: isolatedModules ensures each file can be transpiled independently, which is required by tools like esbuild, SWC, and Babel that process files one at a time. It disallows const enums and namespace merging across files.
